# Welcome to the Pi codebase {#sec:index}

This guide is for developers who are new to Pi's TypeScript codebase; you only
need to be comfortable reading code. Its job is practical: by the end, you
should be able to trace a prompt through Pi, find the right layer for a change,
and build an extension without guessing at the API.

This is a guide to the repository as checked out, not a replacement for the
published user documentation. File paths throughout are repository-relative.
They are deliberately specific so that you can open the implementation beside
this book. When a section simplifies a control flow, it says so; the named
source files are the authority for edge cases.

## Prerequisites and development setup

Before diving into the codebase, make sure your environment is ready.

| Requirement | Minimum version | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Node.js | >= 22.19.0 | Pi runs erasable TypeScript directly with Node's native type stripping and uses modern ESM features. Native stripping does not type-check or transform TypeScript syntax that needs JavaScript emit; `tsx` remains a development runner and `jiti` loads extensions. See the `engines` field in `package.json`. |
| npm | Bundled with Node.js | Workspace management and package installs. The repo uses `npm workspaces`. |
| git | Any recent version | To clone and navigate repository history. |
| TypeScript knowledge | Reading level | This guide teaches what you need in [chapter 1](#sec:typescript). You do not need to be fluent. |

### Quick start

```bash
git clone https://github.com/earendil-works/pi.git
cd pi
npm install --ignore-scripts  # Install dependencies without running lifecycle scripts
npm run build                 # Build all packages
npm run check                 # Lint, format, and type check
./pi-test.sh                  # Run pi from source (works from any directory)
```

`npm install --ignore-scripts` is intentional: the project pins exact dependency
versions and does not run install lifecycle scripts by default. Use `npm ci --ignore-scripts`
for a clean CI-style install only when `package-lock.json` is present and matches
`package.json`; it removes the existing installation and installs the lockfile
exactly. The final command, `./pi-test.sh`, launches the repository's Pi
implementation directly from source rather than a globally installed or
published build.

If you only want to write extensions (not modify Pi itself), you do not need to
clone this repository. Install pi globally and start writing `.ts` files under
`~/.pi/agent/extensions/`. The extension guide in [chapter 4](#sec:extensions)
walks through each step.

### Editor setup

Any editor with TypeScript support works. VS Code users get inline type hints,
autocomplete for `ExtensionAPI` methods, and schema validation on tool parameters
out of the box when TypeScript resolves the `@earendil-works/*` package types. If
you are writing extensions outside the monorepo, install the published packages
as dev dependencies to get editor support:

```bash
npm install --save-dev @earendil-works/pi-ai @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent
```

### TypeScript configuration for extension authors

Extensions are loaded by Pi at runtime with `jiti`, so you do not need a
tsconfig.json or build step for a single-file extension. If you write a
multi-file extension or a Pi package, create a minimal tsconfig.json that
resolves the Pi package types:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ESNext",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "strict": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true
  }
}
```

Type-check your extension with `npx tsc --noEmit`.

## What Pi is

Pi is a TypeScript monorepo for an agent harness and a self-extensible coding
agent. The repository is an npm workspaces project: its root `package.json`
declares the workspaces. The interactive `pi` command is implemented by
`@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`; it composes a terminal UI, an agent runtime,
and a provider-neutral AI API.

### How the npm workspace works

The root `package.json` declares five package workspaces through `"packages/*"`:
`packages/tui`, `packages/ai`, `packages/agent`, `packages/coding-agent`, and
`packages/orchestrator`. It also declares five explicit extension-example
workspaces: `with-deps`, `custom-provider-anthropic`, `custom-provider-gitlab-duo`,
`sandbox`, and `gondolin` under `packages/coding-agent/examples/extensions/`.
Thus, `packages/*` is not the complete workspace list.

`npm install` at the repository root installs dependencies for every declared
workspace and links local workspace packages for package-name imports such as
`@earendil-works/pi-ai`. Those imports use each workspace's package metadata
(the published entries point at `dist`); the root `tsconfig.json` separately maps
package names to source entry points for whole-project type checking without
compiling.

When you write an extension outside the monorepo, you import from the published
npm packages (`@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`, etc.). Pi bundles these at
runtime, so you specify them as `peerDependencies` (not `dependencies`) to avoid
bundling a duplicate copy.

### Import rules at a glance

| Context | Import style | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Inside a package, same package | Relative with `.ts` | `import { Agent } from "./agent.ts"` |
| Inside the repo, cross-package | Package name (no `.ts`) | `import { Type } from "@earendil-works/pi-ai"` |
| Extension outside the repo | Published package name | `import { defineTool } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent"` |

The four packages on the normal interactive prompt path are:

| Package | Role | Start reading at |
|---|---|---|
| `packages/tui` | Reusable terminal UI primitives and differential rendering | `src/index.ts`, `src/tui.ts` |
| `packages/ai` | Models, provider dispatch, streaming API adapters, authentication primitives | `src/index.ts`, `src/types.ts`, `src/compat.ts` |
| `packages/agent` | Provider-independent agent loop, message state, tool execution | `src/agent.ts`, `src/agent-loop.ts`, `src/types.ts` |
| `packages/coding-agent` | CLI, interactive application, sessions, built-in tools, configuration, extensions | `src/main.ts`, `src/core/sdk.ts`, `src/core/agent-session.ts` |

`packages/orchestrator` is an experimental package depending on the coding
agent. It is useful when you specifically need its process orchestration
surface, but it is not on the normal interactive prompt path described here.

## A mental model in one diagram

```text
terminal input
    |
    v
coding-agent interactive mode
    |
    v
AgentSession: commands, sessions, resources <---- extension hooks: input,
    |                                               session, UI, requests
    v
agent-core: context, streaming, tools <---------- extension hooks: tools,
    |                                               turn lifecycle
    |
    v
pi-ai: model dispatch and provider stream
    |
    v
provider API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, ...)
```

The vertical arrows show the usual prompt path. Extensions are loaded by the
coding agent and hook into defined AgentSession and agent-loop boundaries—for
example to inspect input, observe session events, add or wrap tools, customize
UI, or alter requests. These hooks participate in the normal runtime; they do
not bypass the agent loop.

## Reading order and outcomes

Read chapters 1–5 in order for the shortest path from repository orientation to
a working customization:

1. [TypeScript used by Pi](#sec:typescript) makes the recurring type
   syntax and module conventions readable.
2. [Architecture: packages, ownership, and entry points](#sec:architecture)
   maps package boundaries and runtime entry points.
3. [Prompt lifecycle: from Enter to the next render](#sec:prompt-lifecycle)
   follows one prompt through the major runtime boundaries.
4. [Extensions: change Pi without forking it](#sec:extensions) explains
   discovery, hooks, tools, UI, state, packaging, and a minimal extension.
5. [Customization patterns and contributor workflow](#sec:customization)
   helps you choose configuration, an extension, SDK use, or a core change.

Then continue through the subsystem chapters and source exercises as your work
requires:

6. [Sessions, branches, and durable context](#sec:sessions) covers
   persistence, branching, compaction, and extension state.
7. [Resources, settings, and project trust](#sec:resources)
   explains startup inputs, precedence, discovery, and trust boundaries.
8. [Built-in tools: filesystem and shell execution](#sec:tools)
   describes tool registration, execution, cancellation, and safety behavior.
9. [Models, providers, streaming, and authentication](#sec:models)
   separates model metadata, provider adapters, credentials, and dispatch.
10. [Agent core: runs, events, queues, and tool batches](#sec:agent-core)
    details the reusable loop beneath the coding agent.
11. [Terminal UI and interactive mode](#sec:tui) maps TUI
    primitives to the coding-agent interaction layer.
12. [Testing, debugging, and contribution workflow](#sec:testing)
    shows how to localize failures and choose the right validation path.
13. [Guided source tours and architecture exercises](#sec:tours)
    turns the architecture into repeatable code-reading exercises.

Chapters 14–16 are concise references to consult while implementing or
troubleshooting:

14. [Extension event and API reference](#sec:events) helps
    select hooks by representation, timing, return value, and lifecycle.
15. [Run modes, SDK embedding, and operational boundaries](#sec:modes)
    compares interactive, print, JSON, RPC, and SDK hosts.
16. [Failure and recovery reference](#sec:failure) maps
    symptoms and failure data to the responsible layer and recovery behavior.

The [glossary](#sec:glossary) is a quick reference for terms that recur across
the codebase.

## Source map used for this guide

The guide was checked against the package manifests, the extension examples,
the coding-agent extension documentation, and these implementation seams:

- CLI composition: `packages/coding-agent/src/main.ts` and
  `src/core/agent-session-services.ts`.
- Session construction and prompt preprocessing:
  `packages/coding-agent/src/core/sdk.ts` and `src/core/agent-session.ts`.
- Low-level loop and tool execution: `packages/agent/src/agent.ts`,
  `src/agent-loop.ts`, and `src/types.ts`.
- Provider dispatch: `packages/ai/src/compat.ts`, `packages/ai/src/models.ts`,
  and `packages/ai/src/types.ts`.
- Extension contract and loading: `packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/`
  plus `packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md`.

The repository evolves. If a symbol named in this book moves, use
`rg "symbolName" packages` from the repository root, then update your mental
model from the current implementation rather than preserving an old path.
